One day, they awoke simultaneously, their eyes meeting in the grey void. Solara laughed, and her joy burst forth as golden light, painting the sky with the first dawn. Where her light touched the ground, flowers bloomed in riots of red and yellow. Umbra, startled by the brightness, sighed, a sound that became the first wind. He waved his hand, weaving shadows beneath the trees and cooling the air with silver moonlight.

They realized that one could not exist beautifully without the other. Solara’s heat was too intense without Umbra’s shade; Umbra’s darkness was terrifying without Solara’s light. They began to dance—a swirling waltz of day and night. When Solara led, the sun reigned; when Umbra dipped her low, the stars emerged. Thus, the cycle of time was born not from conflict, but from the eternal dance of two spirits learning to share the sky.

She loved her voice so much that she would interrupt the gods themselves just to hear her own melodic laughter.

One day, the great goddess Hera descended to the forest, seeking quiet contemplation. But Calista, seeing an audience, began to sing, mimicking the birds so loudly that Hera could not think. Enraged by the interruption, Hera cursed the nymph. "Since you love the sound of your own voice above all others, and since you must always have the last word, so shall it be forever. You will never speak first, but you shall always repeat the last thing spoken to you."

Calista’s form faded into the rocky cliffs and deep caves. She became a spirit of stone and air. Now, she waits in the canyons and empty halls. When a traveler shouts out, hoping for connection, it is lonely Calista who answers back, faithfully repeating their words, forever longing to tell her own story but only able to mirror theirs.
